---
title: "Varieties as Conditioning Structure"
subtitle: "A Game-Theoretic and Bayesian Framework"
author: "Brett Reynolds"
institute: "Humber Polytechnic & University of Toronto"
date: "LVC Study Group -- 27 March 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../../.house-style/quarto-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    transition: none
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.06
    self-contained: true
---

## A syntactician walks into a study group...

Most of my work has been in English syntax, not LVC, but recently I've been working on some ideas from the philosophy of science that I wanted to share here -- LVC is so important to understanding syntax, and it's a good test case for these ideas. I want to know if this is useful to you or if I'm missing something obvious.

::: {.notes}
Set expectations. This grew out of my HPC book project, where I needed a principled account of how variety categories work. I'm bringing tools from philosophy of science -- game theory and Bayesian learning -- to concepts you use every day. I want genuine pushback from people who know variation better than I do.
:::

## The claim

::: {.r-fit-text}
Register, dialect, and discourse community are three **anchors** for the same judgment.
:::

The framework: varieties as **conditioning structure** -- stable patterns in what forms are appropriate given what.

::: {.notes}
Open with the core claim. The standard move in sociolinguistics is to define these by external correlates: dialects are geographic or social, registers are functional or situational. But these criteria often conflict. A "legal variety" might be a register or a discourse community. Multicultural Toronto English might be a dialect or a set of community-anchored registers. I'm proposing we ask a different question: what is appropriateness indexed to? A note on "appropriateness": I'm using this as a model-internal term for what listeners compute, not as a normative claim that any form is actually inappropriate. The whole point of the framework is to make visible how those judgments produce inequity.
:::

## Two sources

**Wiese (2023):** Grammar lives in [communicative situations]{.term}, not named languages. Varieties describe regularities across situations.

**O'Connor (2019):** Linguistic choices solve [coordination problems]{.term}. What stabilises is what coordinates, not what clusters naturally.

**Combined:** Varieties are stable partitions of situation-space, shaped by coordination pressures.

::: {.notes}
Two theoretical pillars. Wiese provides the ontology: the primary unit is the communicative situation (com-sit), not the named language. "Free-range" settings -- urban markets, heritage speakers, multiethnic peer groups -- make this visible because named-language ideologies are weak there. O'Connor provides the dynamics: why do certain partitions stabilise? Because they solve coordination problems. Her key insight from evolutionary game theory: terms track payoff space, not property space. Even when property clusters exist, the categories that evolve may not map to them -- they track what matters for successful interaction.
:::

## The three anchors

| Anchor | Conditions on | Traditional label |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| [Situation]{.term} | Activity, setting, goals | Register |
| [Ascription]{.term} | How you're classified | Dialect |
| [Identification]{.term} | Whose norms you orient to | Discourse community |

Same judgment type. Different conditioning variable.

::: {.notes}
Here's the framework laid out. All three are dimensions of the same thing -- appropriateness. They differ in what that appropriateness is indexed to. People who know the three-waves literature better than I do may see a mapping here -- ascription aligning roughly with first-wave demographic conditioning, identification with third-wave attention to social meaning and stance. I'd be curious whether that mapping holds up or oversimplifies.
:::

## Unpacking the anchors

**Situation:** Observable context -- what we're doing, where, with what stakes. Shifts rapidly, even within a conversation.

**Ascription:** Social classification by others -- how listeners place you, based on perceived background, history, group membership. Not chosen. Durable.

**Identification:** Normative orientation -- whose approval you seek, whose standards you hold yourself to. Chosen (within constraints). Negotiable but sticky.

::: {.notes}
These differ in stability. Situation is token-level -- it shifts within a day, within a conversation, sometimes within an utterance. And it's partly constructed by participants: people ratify roles, renegotiate footing. Ascription is speaker-level -- durable, tracking features others treat as stable. Ascriptions can be contested and may shift over biographical time, but they have material consequences because others condition their behaviour on them. Identification is trajectory-level -- you can change who you orient to, but such changes are effortful and socially consequential. This maps onto the traditional intuitions: registers shift fast, dialects are stable, communities are chosen but hard to switch.
:::

## The interesting space

::: {.r-fit-text}
Ascription and identification can **align** -- or they can **diverge**.
:::

When they align: forms appropriate to your ascribed variety also signal your identification. No tension.

When they diverge: you're navigating between how you're heard and who you're orienting to.

**That gap is where style-shifting, accommodation, and "passing" live.**

::: {.notes}
This is the key insight. A working-class kid in law school has an ascribed position (class background, regional origin) but identifies with a new community (legal profession). Their speech reflects both: the ascribed baseline modulated by the identificational target. Similarly, a speaker from a multiethnic Toronto neighbourhood may be ascribed to MTE but identify differently depending on context -- embracing it with friends, orienting toward Standard Canadian English with a teacher, code-switching fluidly across the day. The gap between ascription and identification is a major locus of sociolinguistic agency.
:::

## A case: ascription overrides identification

I lived in Japan for ten years. Japanese spouse, bilingual children.

In restaurants, wait staff would ask my wife what I wanted. I'd reply in Japanese. They'd follow up with her.

. . .

My **identification** was fully oriented to the Japanese-speaking community. My **ascription** overrode the signal. The listener couldn't update on the evidence because ascription was doing all the conditioning work.

::: {.notes}
This is the ascription-identification gap made personal. My identification history was thoroughly Japanese-oriented: decade of residence, Japanese family, daily life in Japanese. But ascription -- how listeners classified me on sight -- was so strong it blocked the uptake of the actual linguistic signal. This connects to what critical sociolinguists call raciolinguistic perception: the perception is prior to the signal. The framework gives that insight a formal address rather than treating it as an embarrassment for the model.
:::

## Identification shapes production

I learned much of my Japanese from women -- friends, students, my wife. My default first person is [boku]{.mention}, not [ore]{.mention}. I never acquired the particle [sa]{.mention}. I tend to be more casual than the situation might warrant.

. . .

My production reflects whose norms I tracked -- that's the prior doing its work.

The same values show up in English: I tell my EAP students to call me "Brett," not "Professor Reynolds." Identification can be consistent across languages even when ascription differs radically.

::: {.notes}
Two layers here. First, identification history shapes production distributions. My Japanese reflects whose norms I tracked during acquisition -- the women I learned from had different distributions from the stereotypical male speaker. Second, the anti-hierarchy preference operates across languages: same values, different ascriptive contexts. Identification encompasses whose values you're enacting, not only whose community you're in. And both layers collide with ascription: a white guy producing boku-inflected, casually-registered Japanese gets heard through the foreigner filter before any of these nuances land.
:::

## How learners track this: the Bayesian story

The game theory explains **why** certain partitions stabilise.

The Bayesian account explains **how** learners track them.

Core idea: learners track which forms are appropriate in which circumstances and update with experience.

S/A/I are the conditioning variables that tell the listener **which distribution to compare against**.

::: {.notes}
The two theoretical layers are complementary: games explain population-level dynamics; Bayes explains individual learning. The learner's task is to figure out: given the situation, the speaker's social position, and whose norms seem to be in play, how likely is each variant? The formal version says: for each utterance, the probability of a form depends on situation, ascription, and identification together. Each contributes to the expected distribution. Situation selects register norms; ascription and identification select variety norms. The Bayesian computation is universal; S/A/I parameterise it.
:::

## All three matter at once

The full model conditions on situation, ascription, and identification together.

- Hold A and I constant, vary S: you see **register** variation
- Hold S and I constant, vary A: you see **dialect** variation
- Hold S and A constant, vary I: you see **identification** effects

Register, dialect, and discourse community are slices of one joint distribution, not three separate models.

::: {.notes}
This is the formal point without the notation. The three dimensions aren't independent -- they interact. The interesting theoretical claims (the ascription-identification gap, style-shifting as navigation) live in the interactions. For quantitative work, this means tokens from the same speaker aren't independent; speakers with similar ascriptions share structure; situational effects operate within these nested levels. I have backup slides with the formal notation if anyone wants to see the Bayesian model written out.
:::

## Indexicality as inverse conditioning

If certain forms are more likely in certain contexts, then hearing a form tells you something about the context.

That's just Bayes' theorem running in reverse.

- Hearing [ore]{.mention} suggests a casual, male-peer **situation**
- Hearing [boku]{.mention} from someone who looks like me suggests a foreigner **ascription** (even though it shouldn't)
- Choosing [boku]{.mention} over [ore]{.mention} claims alignment with a particular **identification** network

**Social meaning is the informational flip-side of production conditioning.**

::: {.notes}
Indexicality falls out of the conditioning framework directly. If speakers condition form choice on S/A/I, listeners can invert the conditioning to infer S/A/I from the form. The Japanese first-person pronoun system is a good example because the same form carries situational, ascriptive, and identificational information simultaneously. A single variant supports a field of partially compatible inferences rather than a one-to-one mapping -- context selects which region of that field is foregrounded.
:::

## Anchoring can shift

**Quotative [like]{.mention}** -- a trajectory I lived through:

1. **Identification-indexed:** In the 1980s, marked orientation to a particular youth culture
2. **Situational conditioning develops:** By the 1990s--2000s, conditioned on casual vs. formal register, across demographic groups
3. **Ascription-like durability:** Now a baseline feature of North American English. Speakers aren't "identifying with" anyone -- it's just how quotation works

. . .

**Japanese [だよね]{.mention}** -- the reverse trajectory:

1. **Ascription norm:** Women shouldn't use the assertive copula [だ]{.mention}
2. **Identification as violation:** Young women adopt it in the 1990s; the DA.YO.NE pop hit (1994) makes it hyper-salient
3. **Situational neutralisation:** Now unmarked casual speech across genders

**The framework predicts both directions. Anchoring can shift any way.**

::: {.notes}
These two cases show opposite trajectories through the same S/A/I space. Quotative like moved from identification to situation to ascription -- diffusion outward from an in-group. Japanese だよね moved from ascriptive norm (gendered particle ideology) through noticed violation to situational neutralisation. The framework doesn't privilege any particular trajectory; it just says anchoring can shift. The analytical work is the same: at any moment, you ask which anchor dominates appropriateness judgments for a particular form, and you can track how that changes. Okamoto (1995) documents the broader pattern of young Japanese women adopting "moderately masculine" sentence-final forms, of which だよね is a prime example. I was living in Japan during this shift and remember the DA.YO.NE song making the form culturally hyper-salient.
:::

## What this reframes

| Old framing | New framing |
|-------------|-------------|
| Code-switching between X and Y | Shift in conditioning anchor |
| Style as individual expression | Coordination with a reference group |
| Dialect as property of speaker | Ascription by listeners |
| Register as property of situation | What situation licenses |

The categories don't disappear. They get reanalysed as dimensions of conditioning.

::: {.notes}
Emphasise that I'm not dissolving the categories. Register, dialect, and discourse community remain useful working concepts -- they pick out real patterns that matter for sampling, stratification, and analysis. What the framework adds is a principled account of what makes them different. If you're studying regional variation, you're conditioning on ascription. If you're studying style-shifting, you're looking at how situation modulates an ascribed baseline. If you're studying professional socialisation, you're tracking how identification shifts. The framework doesn't dissolve these -- it tells you what you're doing when you make them.
:::

## Agency

::: {.r-fit-text}
Ascription is imposed. Identification is (partially) chosen.
:::

The gap between them is a major locus of speaker agency.

My casual Japanese register and my "call me Brett" are the same move: identification pushing back against ascription-based hierarchy, in two different languages.

::: {.notes}
This is where the framework does real theoretical work. My casualness in Japanese reflects the same egalitarian values I enact in English, not just a proficiency artefact. The ascription differs radically (white foreigner in Japan, white professor in Toronto), but the identification is consistent: orient away from hierarchy. The framework captures this cross-linguistic consistency because identification is about values and normative orientation, not just community membership.
:::

## The dark side

A listener hears a non-standard form and something shifts -- in expectations, evaluation, behaviour.

That shift is an appropriateness judgment, conditioned on inferred ascription.

When everyone makes similar judgments, they become **coordination signals**.

"Standard" vs "non-standard" = stable equilibrium. Self-sustaining. Hard to exit unilaterally.

::: {.notes}
I'm not telling you that standardisation is unjust -- you know that better than I do. What I'm offering is a formal account of why it's self-sustaining. In O'Connor's framework, once "standard" and "non-standard" become salient categories, they function as coordination signals. Employers coordinate on using "standard" speech as a hiring filter -- not because any individual employer decided to discriminate, but because everyone expects everyone else to use this signal. The equilibrium is self-sustaining. Even employers who know the linguistic facts face pressure to conform. A caveat: the categories that structure this coordination have deep histories. The prestige hierarchy was built by colonialism, state formation, mass education. The game-theoretic point is that once these categories are salient, they sustain themselves through coordination -- but the framework doesn't explain how they got salient. History does that. Ascription categories are historically produced, not just inputs to a model.
:::

## Not fatalism

::: {.r-fit-text}
This is a diagnosis, not an excuse.
:::

Coordination equilibria are stable, but they're not immutable.

Change requires changing payoffs or changing what's conditioned on -- not just changing attitudes.

The framework makes visible *where* intervention could work: shift conditioning away from [ascription]{.term} toward [situation]{.term} (what the context actually requires) and [identification]{.term} (what people have agency over).

::: {.notes}
This validates what critical sociolinguists have argued: individual attitude change is insufficient without structural change. Exhorting fairness doesn't shift equilibria. Anonymising applications, changing institutional policies, removing the signals coordination locks onto -- these change the game itself. The framework suggests two criteria for "good" conditioning: toward properties that are situation-relevant (not arbitrary sorting), and toward properties that are not ascribed (things people have agency over). We can keep teaching Standard varieties to those who want to learn them -- offering repertoire expansion is consistent with valuing identification. The problem is conditioning access on already having it.
:::

## What this predicts

The framework generates testable claims:

1. **Anchor-shift trajectories are ordered.** Forms spreading outward from an in-group should develop situational conditioning before approaching ascription-like durability -- not skip stages
2. **Ascription-identification divergence has a distributional signature.** Speakers whose A and I diverge should show production patterns systematically intermediate between their ascription baseline and their identification target -- not random mixing
3. **Structural interventions outperform attitude campaigns.** Changing what's conditioned on (anonymising, removing signals) should reduce inequity more than exhorting fairness -- because equilibria respond to payoff changes, not beliefs

::: {.notes}
These are predictions that distinguish this framework from alternative accounts. The first predicts an ordered diffusion trajectory that can be tested against corpus data. The second predicts a specific distributional signature for speakers navigating the A/I gap, testable with production data and independent measures of identification. The third is a policy prediction, harder to test but in principle distinguishable from attitude-change models. The contribution is conceptual, but the framework generates specific empirical predictions that alternatives don't.
:::

## Summary

1. Varieties are conditioning structures
2. Register, dialect, discourse community = three anchors for the same judgment
3. The ascription--identification gap is analytically central
4. Bayesian learning explains how individuals track these dimensions
5. Inequity stabilises through coordination, not just prejudice

::: {.notes}
Recap the five key points. The framework offers a way to see how concepts already in use relate to each other and what that means for practice. The S/A/I distinction may also connect to the three-waves trajectory -- I'd be interested in whether people who work in that tradition see the mapping as illuminating or oversimplifying.
:::

## Discussion {.center}

Does the S/A/I distinction help with phenomena you work on?

Where does it break?

What predictions does it make about your data that alternative frameworks don't?

::: {.notes}
Three specific provocations. First: is this useful? Does it clarify things you're already studying, or is it just relabelling? Second: where does it fail? Cases where the three dimensions can't be pulled apart, or where the framework makes wrong predictions. Third: the hardest question. If someone can show me a case where the framework makes the same predictions as existing accounts, it's not earning its keep. I want to know where it generates novel expectations -- and especially where those expectations might be wrong. Also a methodological challenge I'd like help with: situation is tractable (code it from observable features), ascription is harder but doable (group by region, class, etc.), but identification is the challenge. If identification is inferred from the very linguistic choices being modelled, the analysis is circular. Independent measures are needed: network structure, self-report, stance markers, participation histories. What would that look like in your work?
:::

## Backup: formal notation {visibility="hidden"}

The joint distribution over forms, conditioned on all three anchors:

$$Y \mid S{=}s,\, A{=}a,\, I{=}i \;\sim\; \text{Categorical}(\theta_{s,a,i})$$

Marginalise over A and I to recover register variation. Marginalise over S and I to recover dialect variation. The three "variety types" are slices of one joint distribution.

::: {.notes}
Backup slide for anyone who wants the formal notation. The key point is that the three dimensions interact -- you need the joint distribution, not three independent marginals. This is why the framework predicts specific distributional signatures for speakers whose ascription and identification diverge: their production reflects the joint conditioning, not a simple switch between two marginal distributions.
:::
